markdown:
## Additive Manufacturing


<definition term="Additive Manufacturing">**Additive manufacturing** involves **techniques that build objects** by **adding material layer by layer**.</definition>

<callout type="note">The most common form of additive manufacturing is **3D printing**, used to produce prototypes, tools, and even end-use parts.</callout>

### Common 3D Printing Techniques


  1. Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM)
  2. Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)
  3. Stereolithography (SLA)
  4. Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)

#### Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM)


<definition term="Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM)">**LOM **is a **system that slices a 3D CAD model into thin layers**, then **cuts each layer from material** using a **laser or plotter cutter**. The **layers are glued together** in order to form a **3D model**.</definition>

How it works:

1. **Material Layering**: Sheets of material (usually paper, plastic, or metal) are **layered one on top of the other**.
2. **Bonding**: Each layer is **bonded** to the previous one using **heat and pressure**.
3. **Cutting**: A **laser or blade** cuts the desired shape from each layer.
4. **Stacking**: The process repeats until the object is complete.

![Laminate Object Manufacturing](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/9dc08eae9db0b206c52f46427009e960b023d354-1200x843.png)

| Advantages | Limitations |
| --- | --- |
| Cost-Effective: Uses inexpensive materials like paper. | Surface Finish: Rougher compared to other methods. |
| Large-Scale Production: Suitable for creating large objects. | Material Limitations: Limited to sheet-based materials. |
| Minimal Waste: Excess material can often be reused. | Complexity: Less suitable for intricate designs. |

<callout type="note">LOM is often used for **prototyping **and **architectural models**, where cost and speed are prioritised over surface finish.</callout>

#### Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)


<definition term="Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)">**FDM (Fused Deposition Modeling)** is a **3D printing method** that **builds models by depositing melted material** in **layers onto a bed**.</definition>

How it works:

1. **Material Extrusion**: A spool of thermoplastic filament is fed into a heated nozzle.
2. **Layer Deposition**: The nozzle moves along the X, Y, and Z axes, depositing material in precise layers.
3. **Cooling and Solidification**: The material cools and solidifies, forming the object.

| Advantages | Limitations |
| --- | --- |
| Wide Material Range: Supports materials like PLA, ABS, and PETG. | Surface Finish: Visible layer lines may require post-processing. |
| Accessibility: Widely available and affordable for hobbyists and professionals. | Strength: Layer adhesion can be a weak point in some designs. |
| Versatility: Suitable for prototyping, functional parts, and educational purposes. | Speed: Slower compared to other methods for complex geometries. |

![Fused Deposition Modelling (ultratech3Dprinting)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/0a0d865bd00028e76c394cdc8b0624e1cc3fa9e8-2560x1608.png)

<callout type="tip">When using FDM, consider the **orientation **of the object to optimise **strength **and **surface quality**.</callout>

### Stereolithography (SLA)


<definition term="Stereolithography (SLA)">SLA is an **additive manufacturing technique** that **builds 3D models layer by layer** by **hardening a photosensitive liquid polymer** using a **laser beam**.</definition>

How it works:

1. **Resin Vat**: The object is built in a vat (tank) of liquid photopolymer resin.
2. **Laser Curing**: A UV laser traces the design, curing the resin layer by layer.
3. **Layer Building**: The build platform lowers, and the process repeats until the object is complete.

![Stereolithography (3Dnatives) ](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/c82313996b6c3acf4f9b2f32a9b694e9fce2469c-1280x720.png)

| Advantages | Limitations |
| --- | --- |
| High Precision: Produces smooth surfaces and intricate details. | Cost: More expensive than FDM due to resin and equipment costs. |
| Material Variety: Offers resins with diverse properties (e.g., flexible, rigid, biocompatible). | Post-Processing: Requires cleaning and curing of printed parts. |
| Applications: Ideal for dental models, jewelry, and high-detail prototypes. | Material Constraints: Limited to photopolymer resins. |

<callout type="analogy">* Think of SLA as **sculpting with light**. 
* The laser acts like a chisel, carving out the design from a pool of liquid resin.</callout>

### Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)


<definition term="Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)">**SLS** is an **additive manufacturing technique** that **uses a laser to fuse small particles** of material into a **solid 3D shape** for **rapid prototyping**.</definition>

![Selective Laser Sintering (protolabs network)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/71672c1f002b038368d9c228a2898eeb0cdf3e11-1200x675.png)

| Advantages | Limitations |
| --- | --- |
| No support structures needed - the surrounding unsintered powder acts as a support, allowing for complex geometries and overhangs. | Rough surface finish - parts often come out with a grainy texture and may require post-processing (e.g., sanding or polishing). |
| High mechanical strength - produces durable, functional parts. | High equipment cost - SLS printers are expensive and require specialised environments. |
| Efficient material use - unused powder can often be recycled and reused. | Limited to specific materials - primarily used with nylon and polymer powders.  |

### Emerging 3D Printing Techniques


1. **Digital Light Processing (DLP)**: Similar to SLA but uses a digital light projector for faster curing.
2. **Multi-Material Printing**: Combines different materials in a single print, enabling functional and aesthetic versatility.

### Applications of Additive Manufacturing


1. **Prototyping**: Rapid iteration and testing of designs.
2. **Healthcare**: Custom prosthetics, dental models, and surgical guides.
3. **Aerospace**: Lightweight components and complex geometries.
4. **Consumer Products**: Customizable goods and on-demand manufacturing.

<callout type="example">In the aerospace industry, companies like **Airbus **use additive manufacturing to produce lightweight components, reducing fuel consumption and emissions.</callout>
